title: Report on Grant Consolidation Authority for Puerto Rico Act
short_title: Report on Grant Consolidation Authority for Puerto Rico Act
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.
plain_summary: This bill tells the Government Accountability Office to study how US territories can combine their grant applications into one big application instead of filling out many separate ones. Right now, some US territories can do this, but Puerto Rico cannot. The study will look at whether Puerto Rico should be allowed to use this same process to make getting federal money easier. Puerto Rico officials must help by answering questions and providing information for the study. The study must be finished within one year.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Report on Grant Consolidation Authority for Puerto Rico Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Congressional Findings and Statement of Purpose
sections.text: Congress has found that Puerto Rico faces serious financial problems. These include the biggest city bankruptcy in United States history, higher poverty rates than the rest of the country, and fewer people working. Puerto Rico is also rebuilding after major disasters like hurricanes Irma, Maria, and Fiona, earthquakes in two thousand twenty, and the COVID pandemic. These disasters have put a heavy load on Puerto Rico's government workers. They must follow new money rules set by a Financial Oversight and Management Board under a law called PROMESA. They must also handle large amounts of federal disaster money, which means working with new agencies and meeting new requirements. At the same time, they face limits on spending while trying to hire skilled workers to use the disaster funding properly. The purpose of this law is to study whether combining different grant programs would help reduce the burden on Puerto Rico's government and help them better access federal funding.

sections.title: Section 3 Study Required
sections.text: Within one year after this law is passed, the Comptroller General of the United States (the head of an agency that studies government programs) must give a report to certain congressional committees. The report must study how the federal government combines grants for insular areas (territories like Guam and the Virgin Islands) under part ninety seven of title forty five of the Code of Federal Regulations (government rules). The report must include several things. First, it must analyze how local agencies in Puerto Rico currently get funding from programs listed in section ninety seven point twelve of those regulations, working with Puerto Rico officials to understand this. Second, it must list any grant programs that Puerto Rico officials think should be added to that list. Third, it must describe any problems the Comptroller General or Puerto Rico officials have noticed in meeting the current requirements to get funding from these programs. Fourth, it must assess whether any of these problems would be partly or completely solved by letting Puerto Rico combine funding in the same way that insular areas can. Fifth, it must include any recommendations from Puerto Rico officials about how the current process for getting funding should change, including recommendations about letting Puerto Rico combine funding. Any Puerto Rico official that the Comptroller General asks for information must respond quickly and completely, no later than ninety days after getting the request. If someone does not respond or gives incomplete information, the Comptroller General can view this negatively when writing the report. In this section, appropriate congressional committees means the Committee on Natural Resources, the Committee on Education and Labor, and the Committee on Energy and Commerce in the House of Representatives, and the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources and the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions in the Senate.
